## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about using chat and questions in your webinar. So there's a perennial question that goes around a lot in the webinar circles of should you use chats in your webinar. So here's what I'm going to answer. I'm going to give you a definite maybe on that one. I don't have a definitive yes or no answer. And a lot of that is because it all depends on how chat goes because chat can absolutely contribute to your webinar success. And it can harpoon it and you never know until you see how chat is actually happening on a given webinar. So let's just be clear about chat versus questions. So in chat, people can see each other's postings. In questions, only you can see them. Not all services give you the ability to do both, but some of them do and you have to either make a choice or allow people to do both. I've actually seen mixed results chat. As I said, sometimes helps sometimes hurts in webinars. It helps by giving a feeling of community and social proof. And hey, there's a lot of people on here and they're all very interested in this and they're supporting you as the webinar presenter. And when you ask a question and ask people to put in a why if they agree and people just see this flood of 100 why show up, they start going, whoa, everyone else is liking this. And so that can actually contribute to the cells in your webinar. The hurts is that sometimes these chats get off and start talking about all kinds of stuff that has nothing to do with your webinar, or they start debating about something, or someone says, Well, my system's really good. And they actually start selling their system on your webinar. And there's risk that happens here. And so a lot actually depends on what happens in that chat. And obviously, if it's distracting, it can hurt you. The one thing is clear. If you have a very small number of people attending and I'd say under 25, definitely under 50, probably under 100. Yeah, probably. So maybe if you got 100 people or more attending, then you probably don't want to use chat. Here's another thing that's fairly sure. If possible, make chat become private. So you turn off chat when you get to the sales offer part. So only you can see what's the once what they post once the sales offer starts. That allows you to be able to have someone's negative questions or negative comments, not infect the rest of the group negatively. ## [03:01 - 05:35]

this before about handling questions as they go along. But there's a couple of other things I wanted to talk about. So the ideal is to have a staff member assigned to watch the question and chat stream and answer those questions as you go along. This allows the people who ask questions to get their question answered fairly immediately and not have to like have this piece of brain power out there saying, Hey, I want to ask this question at some point later on in the presentation. And so they can be fully present in your presentation. It's almost always a good idea to have a staff person doing that. And I actually talk in a separate video about who that type of person should be. I also suggest that you create an internal script. So not something that goes out to your audience about how you want certain questions to be answered. This not only guides that person who's answering the question in the best way to answer questions, but it also gives them the ability to cut and paste in the ideal answers. In the end, there's a better consumer experience that happens in this process because they get better answers, they get faster answers, and that internal script probably increases cells for you. It's also usually good to have private chat set up on your cells and checkout pages too. So this is not the chat that's happening in your webinar. This is a live chat kind of service, or maybe even a mess Facebook messenger kind of service that's set up on your cells and checkout pages. So if people hit those pages and have questions that are specifically about what it is that you're selling, they can ask and get them answered there. These should be set up and staffed separately so that you can deal with potential buyer questions the best way possible. Now sometimes it's the same person running that chat as well as running the live chat on your webinar. Ideally, if you've got a lot of people on your webinar, I would propose that you have a separate person doing that. But it is a good idea to have that chat service on your checkout pages and sales pages so that the people who have specific product related questions can get them asked and answered even while your webinar is going on.
